title: MotionPaint: Is Motion the Home Advantage of Video Generative Priors?
teaser: Repurpose a pretrained video diffusion model into a single-step, prompt-switched estimator of optical and 3D scene flow — and run the controlled experiment nobody has published: the identical recipe on a video prior versus an image prior, isolating what the video prior's motion curriculum actually contributes.
abstract: Motion is the one thing a video generation model must learn that an image model never sees — this intuition motivates a fast-growing line of work extracting motion from video generative models. Yet the claim has never been isolated: optical flow has been painted with image diffusion models (DDVM), correspondence has been read out of video DiTs zero-shot (DiffTrack), and video DiTs have been fine-tuned into dense 3D trackers (TrackCraft3R) — but no controlled comparison exists of a video prior against an image prior under an identical adaptation recipe, data, and output encoding. This project runs that experiment. We fine-tune a pretrained video diffusion model (WAN 2.1, LoRA) into a single-step, feed-forward motion estimator whose answers are painted as color videos and switched by text prompt — optical flow, 3D scene flow, and rigid/non-rigid motion masks — trained on synthetic data with free ground truth (Kubric, FlyingThings3D, CARLA) plus real scene-flow supervision mined from stereo video (Stereo4D). The matched counterpart applies the same recipe to an image diffusion prior on stacked frames. Hypotheses: (i) the video prior dominates precisely on the tasks requiring temporal integration (scene flow, motion segmentation) and the gap widens as labeled data shrinks; (ii) a single prompt-switched model produces mutually consistent flow and motion masks. Evaluation on Sintel, KITTI, and Spring for 2D flow and dense-3D-tracking protocols for scene flow, against discriminative baselines. The prior-comparison analysis is the contribution that stands regardless of where the benchmark numbers land.
milestones: Literature review (flow painted by diffusion — DDVM; motion extraction from video generative models — DiffTrack, TrackCraft3R; unified video perception — L4P); prepare synthetic training data with flow/scene-flow ground truth (Kubric, FlyingThings3D, CARLA); pass the sanity gates on WAN 2.1 (1.3B) with LoRA: frozen-VAE round-trip of flow color encodings (signed, unbounded vectors are the go/no-go test), identity behavior at step zero, small-set overfit.

2D optical flow arm: train the single-step prompt-switched flow painter and its matched image-prior counterpart (identical recipe, data, and encoding on an image diffusion backbone); benchmark both on Sintel/KITTI/Spring and plot the video-vs-image gap across training-data scales.

3D scene flow arm: extend the painting encoding to 3D flow (direction and magnitude channels), add real supervision from Stereo4D; evaluate under dense-3D-tracking protocols against feed-forward discriminative baselines.

Joint-consistency analysis: add prompt-switched rigid/non-rigid motion masks and measure cross-task consistency of the single model; ablate encoding choices; consolidate the video-vs-image-prior study across all tasks.
(Stretch) Zero-shot transfer study on real videos (motion segmentation benchmarks), or distill the painted outputs into a real-time student.

prerequisites.label: Advanced Deep Learning for Computer Vision: Visual Computing (ADL4CV)

references.label: The Surprising Effectiveness of Diffusion Models for Optical Flow and Monocular Depth Estimation (DDVM)
GenCeption: Video Generation Models are General-Purpose Vision Learners
TrackCraft3R: Repurposing Video Diffusion Transformers for Dense 3D Tracking
L4P: Towards Unified Low-Level 4D Vision Perception
Stereo4D: Learning How Things Move in 3D from Internet Stereo Videos
